case_name: Jandre v. Wisconsin Injured Patients & Families Compensation Fund

summary.brief: Jandre v. Wisconsin Injured Patients & Families Compensation Fund. Medical malpractice case involving paediatrics.

evidence_index.text: The Jandres asserted two claims: (1) Dr. Bullis negligently diagnosed Jandre with Bell's palsy; and (2) Dr. Bullis breached her duty to inform a patient under Wis. Stat. § 448.30 (2007-08), by failing to inform Jandre of a diagnostic test (a carotid ultrasound) that was available to rule out the possibility of a stroke.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The jury found that Dr. Bullis's diagnosis of Bell's palsy was not negligent and also found that Dr. Bullis was negligent with respect to her duty to inform the patient.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On June 13, 2003, the coffee Jandre was drinking began coming out of his nose, and he began drooling and slurring his speech. The left side of his face drooped. He experienced about 20 minutes of dizziness and weakness in his legs.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Jandre was evaluated at the emergency room by Dr. Bullis, who read Jandre's chart, took Jandre's medical, social, and family history, and performed a physical examination.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Bullis's differential diagnosis included 'Bell's Palsy, stroke, TIA, all of those stroke syndromes including ischemic as well as hemorrhagic, tumors, syndromes like— things like Guillain-Barre, MS [multiple sclerosis], and multiple other things like that.'
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Bullis ordered a CT scan, which could rule out a hemorrhagic stroke and brain tumors. The results were normal. A CT scan will not detect an ischemic stroke.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Bullís had the option of ordering a carotid ultrasound to assess the state of Jandre's carotid arteries, but she chose not to.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Three days after seeing Dr. Bullis in the emergency room, Jandre saw a family medicine physician who noted that Jandre exhibited signs of resolving Bell's palsy.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On the evening of June 24, 2003, Jandre suffered a full-blown stroke, which impaired his physical and cognitive abilities.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Two expert witnesses at trial testified that they would have ordered a carotid ultrasound for Jandre on June 13, 2003; that Jandre had experienced a TIA or RIND on that day; that a carotid ultrasound would have revealed the blockage in Jandre's carotid artery; and that surgery could have been performed, reducing the likelihood that Jandre would suffer a stroke.
evidence_index.speaker: Expert witnesses

evidence_index.text: The jury found that Dr. Bullis's decision not to employ carotid ultrasound during her diagnosis of Mr. Jandre's ailment was not negligent.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The claim of informed consent should be dismissed. In addition, because the verdicts for Mr. Jandre's two claims are inconsistent, pursuant to our directive in Westfall, I would order a new trial on the claim of negligent care and treatment.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

initial_state.chief_complaint: Drooling, slurred speech, and facial droop
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: On June 13, 2003, the coffee Jandre was drinking began coming out of his nose, and he began drooling and slurring his speech. The left side of his face drooped. He experienced about 20 minutes of dizziness and weakness in his legs.

focused_exam.neurological: Facial droop, slurred speech, dizziness, and leg weakness noted

requestables.description: CT scan to rule out hemorrhagic stroke and brain tumors

reveal.clinical_significance: Ruled out hemorrhagic stroke and brain tumors
reveal.decision_impact: Did not rule out ischemic stroke

requestables.name: Carotid Ultrasound
requestables.description: Non-invasive test to assess carotid artery blockage

reveal.clinical_significance: Could have identified carotid artery blockage

timeline_phases.name: Emergency Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents to ED with acute neurological symptoms

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient in ED with symptoms suggestive of neurological event
decision_points.prompt: What additional diagnostic test should be considered?

options.clinical_reasoning: More reliable than auscultation for detecting carotid artery blockage

options.description: Rely on auscultation of carotid arteries
options.clinical_reasoning: Auscultation is a poor screening test for carotid artery blockage

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant relied on auscultation of carotid arteries

expected_action_court.description: Carotid ultrasound should have been ordered
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony indicated it was necessary to rule out ischemic stroke

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Auscultation is unreliable for detecting significant carotid artery blockage
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Carotid ultrasound should have been performed to rule out ischemic stroke

key_findings: Dr. Bullis was not negligent in her diagnosis of Bell's palsy

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Carotid ultrasound could have identified carotid artery blockage
